worth_it_explanation: Account structure is the spine of every report a publisher reads and every payment a seller declares, so a tangled hierarchy distorts both without breaking anything visible. When networks, child accounts, and supply partners are organized cleanly, ownership is clear, reporting rolls up correctly, and the entities a publisher declares in sellers.json match the entities that actually transact. The check is a review of the structure already in FreeWheel and the publisher's public transparency files, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific entity to reorganize or retire.
intro: FreeWheel organizes a publisher's business into networks, child accounts, and supply partners, and that structure determines how inventory is owned, how revenue rolls up in reporting, and which entities appear in the public transparency files buyers check. This subcategory is about the shape of that structure rather than the pricing or delivery rules that sit inside it. These checks confirm that the hierarchy reads cleanly enough for reporting and ownership to be unambiguous, and that inactive or duplicate entities have been found and retired before they muddy reporting or weaken supply-chain transparency.

audit_checks.title: The network and entity hierarchy is not organized for clear reporting and ownership
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A hierarchy that grew by accretion rather than design makes revenue roll up to the wrong parent, leaves ownership of a supply partner ambiguous, and forces analysts to reconcile entities by hand. The numbers are not wrong at the impression level, but the structure that aggregates them is, so reporting and the transparency files derived from it inherit the confusion.

educate.base:
FreeWheel arranges a publisher's business as a hierarchy of entities. A network sits at the top, child accounts can represent brands, regions, or distribution partners beneath it, and supply partners represent the external sources whose inventory the publisher sells. That structure is not only an organizing convenience. It decides how revenue rolls up in reporting, who owns a given slice of inventory, and which entity is named when a transaction is declared to buyers.

A hierarchy that grew over time, as new channels, partners, and apps were added without a plan, drifts away from how the business actually works. Revenue from a streaming app may roll up under a parent that no longer owns it. Two child accounts may cover the same brand because one was created for a launch and the other for a migration. A supply partner may sit at the wrong level, so its inventory is counted in a total it should not belong to. None of these breaks ad serving, which is why they persist, but each one makes a report harder to trust and ownership harder to state plainly.

The structure also feeds the publisher's public transparency files. The seller entities a publisher declares in sellers.json are supposed to map cleanly to the entities that actually transact, and the SupplyChain object that travels with each bid request names the nodes in the path. The IAB Tech Lab sellers.json standard requires that each seller entity map to a single, identifiable party, so a hierarchy that blurs ownership inside the ad server produces a sellers.json that blurs it for buyers too.

A few signs point to a hierarchy that needs reorganizing:
  • Revenue in reporting rolls up under a parent that no longer reflects who owns the inventory
  • The same brand, app, or distribution partner appears under more than one entity
  • A supply partner sits at a level that pulls its inventory into a total it does not belong to
  • Analysts reconcile reporting by hand because the entity names do not match how the business is described
You may want to confirm that the hierarchy reads cleanly against how the business is actually organized. One approach is to map each entity to the brand, channel, or partner it represents and flag any place where ownership or roll-up does not match.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher, the hierarchy often spans several apps, channels, and distribution deals, and each may have been added to FreeWheel at a different time. You may want to confirm that each streaming channel and each multichannel video programming distributor (MVPD) deal maps to the entity that genuinely owns its inventory, so revenue rolls up to the right parent and the channel's seller identity is stated cleanly to buyers.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running shoppable or product-adjacent video, the video inventory may sit alongside editorial inventory under entities that were never meant to separate them. You may want to confirm that the commercial video inventory is owned by an entity that reflects its higher-intent audience, so its revenue does not get blended into an editorial total that misrepresents its value.

investigate.intro: Review the network, child account, and supply-partner hierarchy in FreeWheel against how the business is actually organized, and check whether reporting rolls up and ownership reads cleanly.

freewheel_console.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the entity or company structure view and list the network, its child accounts, and the supply partners attached to it.
For each entity, note what brand, channel, app, or distribution partner it represents and which parent it rolls up to.
Flag any entity whose parent no longer reflects who owns its inventory, and any case where one brand or partner appears under more than one entity.
Confirm with the publisher how the business is actually organized, then record each place where the hierarchy and the business description do not match.

sellers_json.label: Public sellers.json file
steps: Open the publisher's sellers.json file and the file of the FreeWheel selling entity that carries its inventory.
Compare the seller entities declared there against the entity hierarchy you mapped in the console.
Note any seller entity that does not map cleanly to a single identifiable owner. The IAB Tech Lab standard requires that mapping.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner maps each FreeWheel entity to the brand, channel, app, or partner it represents and to the parent it rolls up to, then compares that map against how the publisher describes the business and against the seller entities in sellers.json. A hierarchy where every entity has a clear owner, revenue rolls up to the parent that genuinely owns the inventory, and the seller entities map one to one passes. A hierarchy where roll-up, ownership, or the sellers.json mapping diverges from the real business is a fail recorded with the specific entities affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each entity, the brand or partner it represents, and the parent it rolls up to above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the FreeWheel entity hierarchy for {{site_name}} does not map cleanly to how the business is organized, so revenue rolls up under parents that no longer own the inventory and ownership of some entities is ambiguous. The structure also feeds the public sellers.json file, where each seller entity is meant to map to a single identifiable owner, so the confusion inside the ad server carries through to the transparency buyers read. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each network, child account, and supply partner to the brand, channel, or partner it represents, reorganizing any entity whose roll-up or ownership does not match the real business, and confirming that the corrected hierarchy lines up with the seller entities declared in sellers.json.

audit_checks.title: Inactive or duplicate entities have not been identified and retired
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Dormant child accounts and duplicate supply partners left in place add clutter that slows reporting and audits, and a stale seller entity that lingers in sellers.json after a partner relationship ends weakens the very transparency the file is meant to provide. The risk grows when a retired entity still appears as an authorized seller and buyers treat inventory as legitimate that the publisher no longer stands behind.

educate.base:
Entities accumulate in a FreeWheel account the way files accumulate on a desktop. A child account is created for a brand that is later folded into another. A supply partner is added for a test integration that never went live. A second entity is created during a migration and the first is never removed. Each one made sense at the time, and because retiring an entity is rarely urgent, the dormant and duplicate ones stay.

Left in place, these entities cost more than clutter. Reporting has to account for them even when they carry no activity, so analysts spend time confirming that a zero-revenue entity is genuinely dormant rather than broken. A duplicate supply partner splits the record of a single relationship across two entities, so a partner's true contribution is harder to read. The structure becomes harder to reason about with every entity that no longer corresponds to a live part of the business.

The sharper risk sits in the public transparency files. A publisher declares its seller entities in sellers.json, and the entities it authorizes to sell its inventory in ads.txt and app-ads.txt. When a partner relationship ends or an entity is retired inside FreeWheel, the matching line in those public files needs to come down too. A stale seller entity that lingers in sellers.json, or a retired partner still listed as an authorized seller in ads.txt, tells buyers that inventory is legitimate when the publisher no longer stands behind it, which is the opposite of what those files are meant to do.

A few signs point to entities that need retiring:
  • Child accounts or supply partners carry no activity across a sustained recent period and have no planned future use
  • Two entities cover the same brand, app, or partner relationship
  • A seller entity remains in sellers.json after the relationship it represented has ended
  • An authorized-seller line in ads.txt or app-ads.txt points to a partner the publisher no longer works with
You may want to confirm that inactive and duplicate entities have been found and retired, both inside FreeWheel and in the public files that mirror them. One approach is to list every entity, mark each as live, dormant, or duplicate, and check each retirement against the matching sellers.json and ads.txt lines.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher, supply partners turn over as carriage deals and streaming distribution agreements change, so entities that represented past partners can pile up. You may want to confirm that an ended distribution deal has had its FreeWheel entity retired and its line removed from sellers.json and app-ads.txt, so a former partner is not still presented to buyers as an authorized seller of your inventory.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, short-run campaigns and seasonal product partnerships can leave behind entities that were only ever meant to be temporary. You may want to confirm that an entity created for a finished campaign or a discontinued product line has been retired rather than left dormant, so it stops appearing in reporting and in the public seller declarations.

investigate.intro: Review every network, child account, and supply partner for activity and for duplication, then check whether retired entities have also been removed from the public transparency files.

freewheel_console.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the entity or company structure view and list every child account and supply partner under the network.
Pull a report of activity by entity across a sustained recent period and mark each entity as live, dormant, or duplicate.
Flag any two entities that cover the same brand, app, or partner relationship, and confirm with the publisher whether either has a planned future use.
Record each dormant or duplicate entity along with what it represented and when it was last active.

transparency_files.label: Public sellers.json, ads.txt, and app-ads.txt
steps: Open the publisher's sellers.json file and the relevant ads.txt and app-ads.txt files.
For each entity you marked dormant or duplicate in the console, check whether a matching seller entity or authorized-seller line still appears in the public files.
Note every stale line that represents a retired entity or an ended partner relationship. Those need to come down to keep the files accurate.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists every entity, marks each as live, dormant, or duplicate against an activity report, and checks each retired or duplicate entity against the matching lines in sellers.json, ads.txt, and app-ads.txt. A structure where dormant and duplicate entities have been retired inside FreeWheel and removed from the public files passes. A structure carrying dormant or duplicate entities, or one whose public files still list retired entities or ended partners, is a fail recorded with each entity and the stale lines it left behind.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each entity, its activity status, and whether its public seller lines were removed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that inactive and duplicate entities remain in the FreeWheel structure for {{site_name}}, and that some retired entities still appear in the public transparency files. Dormant child accounts and duplicate supply partners add clutter that slows reporting, and a stale seller entity left in sellers.json or a retired partner still listed in ads.txt tells buyers that inventory is authorized when {{site_name}} no longer stands behind it. {{voice_recommend}} listing every entity, marking each as live, dormant, or duplicate against an activity report, retiring the dormant and duplicate ones inside FreeWheel, and removing the matching seller and authorized-seller lines from sellers.json, ads.txt, and app-ads.txt so the public files reflect only live relationships.
